heb.paragraph: The noun synergos (co-worker) in v. 1 describes Paul and his team as ‘God’s co-workers’—a remarkable designation that positions human ministers as partners in God’s reconciling work. The term does not imply equality with God but genuine participation in the divine project.

heb.gloss: in the right hand and in the left
heb.paragraph: The phrase ‘weapons of righteousness in the right hand and in the left’ (v. 7) draws on military imagery: a soldier carried an offensive weapon (sword) in the right hand and a defensive weapon (shield) in the left. Paul’s ‘weapons’ are the instruments of righteousness deployed in the spiritual battle for the gospel.

refs.note: Paul cites the Servant Song’s promise: ‘In the time of my favour I heard you, and in the day of salvation I helped you.’ He applies the prophetic promise to the present: ‘now is the time of God’s favour, now is the day of salvation.’
Paul’s expanded hardship catalogue in ch. 11 provides the detailed biographical content behind the compressed list here.

notes.note: The appeal not to receive God’s grace ‘in vain’ (v. 1) warns against a superficial acceptance that fails to produce transformed living. The Isaiah citation (v. 2) provides urgency: the present is the decisive moment.

The hardship catalogue is Paul’s counter-credential to the super-apostles’ boasting. Where they display eloquence, visions, and letters of recommendation, Paul displays endurance, suffering, and paradoxical weakness-as-strength. The seven paradoxes of vv. 8–10 are the rhetorical climax: ‘dying, and yet we live on; sorrowful, yet always rejoicing; poor, yet making many rich; having nothing, and yet possessing everything.’

Calvin reads the hardship catalogue as the definitive portrait of authentic ministry. The minister’s credentials are not eloquence or learning (though these have their place) but faithful endurance through suffering. Calvin notes the seven paradoxes as a description of the minister’s inner experience: externally poor, internally rich; externally dying, internally alive. This paradoxical existence mirrors the cross itself.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note explains that Paul’s application of Isa 49:8 to the present moment is an eschatological claim: the ‘day of salvation’ prophesied by Isaiah has arrived with the gospel. The double ‘now’ (nyn) emphasises the urgency.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, Pauline Christology (2007) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee reads the hardship catalogue as Paul’s apostolic ‘CV’—the credentials that authenticate his ministry in contrast to the super-apostles’ triumphalistic self-presentation. The seven paradoxes encapsulate the theology of weakness: divine power is displayed through human frailty.

harris.source: Murray J. Harris, 2 Corinthians, NIGTC/EGC (2005) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Harris provides the most detailed structural analysis of the catalogue, identifying three series: (1) nine tribulations in three triads (vv. 4b–5); (2) nine virtues and resources (vv. 6–7a); (3) seven antithetical paradoxes (vv. 8–10). Harris argues that the structure is deliberate and rhetorically sophisticated, building from external affliction through internal resources to the paradoxical synthesis that defines apostolic existence.

hist.context: Paul’s hardship catalogue (vv. 4–10) is the most rhetorically elaborate in his letters, structured in three sets. The first set (vv. 4–5) lists nine afflictions in three triads. The second set (vv. 6–7) lists nine virtues and resources. The third set (vv. 8–10) presents seven paradoxes that capture the essence of apostolic existence. The catalogue serves as Paul’s ‘curriculum vitae’—his credentials are not letters of recommendation but the marks of suffering borne for the gospel. The citation of Isa 49:8 (v. 2) frames the entire ministry as a fulfilment of the Isaianic servant’s mission.

heb.paragraph: The noun splanchna (affections, literally ‘bowels’) in v. 12 describes the seat of deep emotion in ancient anthropology. Paul’s heart is ‘wide open’ toward the Corinthians (v. 11); it is the Corinthians who are ‘withholding their affection’ (v. 12). The emotional appeal is intense and personal: Paul has nothing to hide from them and asks only for reciprocal openness.

refs.note: Paul resumes this emotional appeal after the digression of 6:14–7:1, repeating the plea: ‘Make room for us in your hearts’ (7:2). The two appeals frame the temple-purity passage.

notes.note: Paul’s direct address—‘We have spoken freely to you, Corinthians’—breaks through the formal argument with personal passion. His heart is ‘wide open’ (v. 11); the constriction is on their side (v. 12). The appeal ‘as to my children’ (v. 13) establishes the parental relationship that authorises both tenderness and firmness.

Calvin reads this appeal as evidence that Paul’s rebukes are motivated by affection, not hostility. The pastoral heart must be open even when the congregation’s heart is closed. Calvin applies this to the minister’s calling: persistent love in the face of rejection is the mark of genuine pastoral care.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on splanchna explains that the term refers to the visceral organs (heart, liver, lungs) regarded as the seat of emotion in ancient thought, equivalent to the modern English ‘heart.’

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, Pauline Christology (2007) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee reads this appeal as the emotional centre of the letter: Paul has laid bare his heart; the Corinthians must reciprocate. Fee notes that the direct address by name (‘Corinthians’) is a rare Pauline device (cf. Gal 3:1; Phil 4:15) that signals heightened emotional intensity.

harris.source: Murray J. Harris, 2 Corinthians, NIGTC/EGC (2005) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Harris notes the rhetorical structure: Paul’s openness (v. 11) contrasts with the Corinthians’ constriction (v. 12), and the appeal for reciprocity (v. 13) requests ‘a fair exchange.’ Harris argues that this emotional appeal is not a digression but the relational foundation for the theological exhortation that follows.

hist.context: This brief but emotionally charged appeal interrupts the theological argument with raw pastoral emotion. Paul addresses the Corinthians directly by name (v. 11—one of only two times in the letter) and speaks ‘as to my children’ (v. 13). The appeal for open hearts prepares for the warning about unequal yoking that follows (vv. 14–18).

sections.header: Verses 14–18 — "Do Not Be Yoked Together with Unbelievers"

heb.paragraph: The participle heterozygountes (‘unequally yoked,’ v. 14) draws on Deut 22:10, which prohibits yoking an ox and a donkey together for plowing. Paul applies the agricultural law metaphorically: believers must not enter binding partnerships with unbelievers that compromise their covenant identity. The prohibition is not about social interaction (cf. 1 Cor 5:9–10) but about covenantal commitment—especially partnerships that involve shared religious allegiance.

The name Beliar (v. 15) is an alternate form of Belial, a Hebrew term meaning ‘worthlessness’ or ‘lawlessness.’ In Second Temple Judaism (especially the Dead Sea Scrolls), Belial became a name for the chief of the demonic forces opposed to God. Paul uses the term only here, likely reflecting his familiarity with Jewish apocalyptic tradition. The five rhetorical questions of vv. 14–16 pit light against darkness, Christ against Beliar, and the temple of God against idols.

refs.note: The prohibition against yoking different animals together is the OT source for Paul’s metaphor. The principle is compatibility: elements of fundamentally different natures should not be bound together.
Paul’s earlier declaration that the believer’s body is a ‘temple of the Holy Spirit’ provides the individual counterpart to the corporate temple-language of 2 Cor 6:16.
The promise ‘My dwelling place will be with them; I will be their God, and they will be my people’ is part of the composite OT citation in v. 16, establishing the covenantal basis for holiness.

notes.note: The five rhetorical questions establish an absolute antithesis: righteousness vs. wickedness, light vs. darkness, Christ vs. Beliar, believer vs. unbeliever, God’s temple vs. idols. The pairs are not merely incompatible but ontologically opposed. The prohibition is not about avoiding all contact with unbelievers (cf. 1 Cor 5:9–10) but about forming binding covenantal partnerships that compromise spiritual identity.

The composite OT citation draws from Leviticus, Isaiah, Samuel, and Ezekiel to establish the covenantal promise: God will dwell with his people, and they must maintain holiness. The father/sons-and-daughters promise (v. 18; cf. 2 Sam 7:14) extends the Davidic covenant to all believers, regardless of gender—a notable Pauline innovation.

Calvin interprets the unequal-yoking prohibition as applying primarily to religious partnerships: marriage to unbelievers, participation in pagan worship, and any alliance that compromises the church’s covenant identity. Calvin is careful to note that Paul does not prohibit all social interaction with unbelievers (which would be impossible, cf. 1 Cor 5:10) but binding relationships that entail religious compromise. The temple declaration (v. 16) makes the holiness imperative inescapable: if the community is God’s dwelling, it must maintain the purity befitting God’s presence.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note explains the agricultural metaphor: yoking an ox and a donkey together (Deut 22:10) results in an unworkable combination. Paul applies this to partnerships between believers and unbelievers where shared covenantal commitment is required.

The NET note identifies the composite citation: Lev 26:12 (‘I will walk among you and be your God’); Isa 52:11 (‘Come out from them and be separate’); 2 Sam 7:14/Isa 43:6 (‘I will be a Father to you’). The combination creates a comprehensive covenantal framework.

fee.source: Gordon D. Fee, Pauline Christology (2007) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Fee argues that the passage is authentically Pauline and contextually appropriate: the Corinthians’ closed hearts toward Paul may be connected to their continued entanglements with pagan associations. Fee reads the temple declaration as ecclesiological: the community as a whole is God’s temple, and its corporate holiness is at stake. The ‘coming out’ language (Isa 52:11) is a call to covenant faithfulness, not to social withdrawal.

harris.source: Murray J. Harris, 2 Corinthians, NIGTC/EGC (2005) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Harris defends the passage’s integrity within its present context, arguing that the five antithetical questions are rhetorically connected to the open-hearts appeal of vv. 11–13: the Corinthians cannot open their hearts to Paul while remaining yoked to pagan partnerships. Harris provides the most detailed analysis of the composite OT citation, noting that Paul selects texts from different covenantal contexts (Sinai, Davidic, eschatological) to construct a comprehensive theology of the community as God’s new-covenant temple.

hist.context: This passage has been debated regarding its placement: some scholars consider it a fragment of the ‘previous letter’ mentioned in 1 Cor 5:9, inserted here by a later editor. However, the theological connection to the surrounding context is defensible: Paul has just appealed for open hearts (vv. 11–13) and will resume the appeal in 7:2. The temple-purity exhortation (vv. 14–18) warns against the very entanglements that are closing the Corinthians’ hearts to Paul. The five antithetical questions (vv. 14–16a) build to the declaration that the community is ‘the temple of the living God’ (v. 16b), followed by a composite OT citation (Lev 26:12; Isa 52:11; 2 Sam 7:14; Ezek 37:27).

rows.label: vv.1–2
rows.text: Eschatological urgency: now is the day of salvation

rows.label: vv.11–13
rows.text: Emotional appeal: open wide your hearts

rows.label: vv.16b–18
rows.text: Composite OT citation: covenant promises and holiness demands

lit.note: Chapter 6 juxtaposes Paul’s apostolic credentials (hardship catalogue) with pastoral appeal (open hearts) and covenant holiness (unequal yoking). The paradoxes of vv. 8–10 are among the most memorable statements of the theology of weakness.

themes.note: Holiness dominates as Paul calls for separation from compromising alliances and presents the community as God’s temple.

hebtext.note: The noun synergos (co-worker) in v. 1 describes Paul and his team as ‘God’s co-workers’—a remarkable designation that positions human ministers as partners in God’s reconciling work. The term does not imply equality with God but genuine participation in the divine project.

Paul uses the term only here, likely reflecting his familiarity with Jewish apocalyptic tradition. The five rhetorical questions of vv. 14–16 pit light against darkness, Christ against Beliar, and the temple of God against idols.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: Christ is a key figure in Commending the Ministry.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Commending the Ministry

translations.text: As God’s co-workers we urge you not to receive God’s grace in vain.
Working together with him, then, we appeal to you not to receive the grace of God in vain.
in purity, understanding, patience and kindness; in the Holy Spirit and in sincere love;
by purity, knowledge, patience, kindness, the Holy Spirit, genuine love;

discourse.thesis: Paul appeals not to receive God's grace in vain — the time of salvation is now.

nodes.text: Working together with God, we urge you not to receive grace in vain. Now is the favorable time; now is the day of salvation.
We put no obstacle in anyone's way. In everything we commend ourselves as servants of God: afflictions, hardships, patience, purity, knowledge, love — as dying yet alive, as poor yet enriching many.
Corinthians, our heart is wide open to you! You are not restricted by us but by your own affections. Widen your hearts also.
Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers. What partnership has light with darkness? Come out and be separate. I will be your Father.

coaching.tip: 'We put no obstacle in anyone's way, so that no fault may be found with our ministry' (v. 3). Paul's catalog of hardships (vv. 4-10) proves authenticity: afflictions, hardships, calamities... but also purity, knowledge, patience, kindness. Suffering doesn't disqualify ministry; it validates it. Endurance speaks louder than ease.

'Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers' (v. 14). The image is mismatched animals pulling different directions. Light and darkness, Christ and Belial, temple and idols — these don't combine. The call is to separation, not isolation. 'Come out from among them and be separate' (v. 17, quoting Isaiah 52:11). Holiness requires boundaries.
